pmos

How Computers & the Internet Work — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. A product manager's (PM's) working mental model of what is actually happening inside a computer and across the internet — from a single 0-or-1 bit, up through the processor and operating system, out across the network, and through the complete journey of a web request from browser to server and back. Written for any PM. Every term is defined the first time it appears. Source provenance: curation-consensus across MDN Web Docs, cpu.land, and the Wikipedia computing/networking corpus. 22 verified sources.

What this is (one paragraph)

Underneath every product you manage is a stack of simple ideas repeated at scale: tiny on/off switches become numbers, numbers become instructions, instructions become running software, and software talks to other software across a global network. This primer walks that stack from the bottom up, then follows one web request all the way from your browser to a server and back. The goal is not to make you an engineer. It is to give you an accurate mental model — a picture in your head of how the pieces fit — so that words like "DNS", "the cloud", "latency", and "TLS" stop being magic and start being things you can reason about in a roadmap discussion.

Why a PM should care

You do not need to write code, but you constantly make decisions shaped by how computers and networks really work. Why is the app slow on mobile? Why does a feature that is "basically done" need another week for "infrastructure"? Why does adding a server in another country matter for some users and not others? Without a mental model you are forced to take every technical answer on faith, which makes you easy to over-promise to and hard to partner with. With one, you ask sharper questions, you spot when a "small change" is actually large, and you can weigh cost, speed, and risk in terms an engineer recognizes. The single most useful habit is to picture the path of a request — every hop between the user and the answer is a place where time, money, and failure can hide.

Core concepts (with definitions)

A handful of building blocks recur everywhere. Learn these and the rest of the stack becomes readable.

Bit and byte
A bit is a single 0 or 1 — the smallest unit of information. A byte is a group of eight bits; per Wikipedia, "the byte is a unit of digital information that most commonly consists of eight bits", enough for 256 distinct values (one letter, say). Megabytes and gigabytes are just large piles of bytes.
Hardware
The physical machine. Wikipedia lists it as "the physical parts of a computer, such as the central processing unit (CPU), random-access memory (RAM), motherboard, computer data storage, graphics card, sound card, and computer case".
Software
The instructions that tell the hardware what to do. Software is not a physical thing; it is patterns of bits the hardware reads and acts on.
Network
Two or more computers connected so they can exchange data. The internet is simply the largest network — a network of networks.

How it actually works — inside the computer (the CPU)

At the center of every device is the Central Processing Unit (CPU) — the part that does the actual computing. Its job, per Wikipedia, is that its "electronic circuitry executes instructions of a computer program, such as arithmetic, logic, controlling, and input/output (I/O) operations". It does this through one endlessly repeated loop, the fetch-decode-execute cycle: grab the next instruction, work out what it means, do it, repeat. Wikipedia describes this as the cycle the CPU "follows from boot-up until the computer has shut down in order to process instructions". The practitioner explainer cpu.land puts the running picture plainly: "the CPU continues running its fetch-execute cycle as usual", marching through instructions one after another, billions of times a second. When someone says a chip is "faster", they mostly mean it runs this loop more times per second or does more per turn.

Memory vs storage — the two are not the same

One of the most common PM confusions is treating "memory" and "storage" as synonyms. They are different layers with different jobs. RAM (Random-Access Memory) is the fast, temporary workspace the CPU uses for whatever is running right now; per Wikipedia it "allows data items to be read or written in almost the same amount of time irrespective of the physical location" — but it forgets everything when power is lost. Storage (a hard drive or solid-state drive) is slower but keeps data permanently. Wikipedia draws the line directly: "the faster and volatile storage components are referred to as memory, while slower persistent components are referred to as storage". The everyday analogy: RAM is your desk (small, fast, cleared each night); storage is the filing cabinet (large, slower, permanent). When an app is "running out of memory" it has filled the desk; when it is "out of storage" the cabinet is full. These are different problems with different fixes.

The operating system — the manager in the middle

Dozens of programs want the CPU, the memory, the network, and the screen at once. The Operating System (OS) — Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android — is the referee. Wikipedia defines it as "system software that manages computer hardware and software resources, and provides common services for computer programs". It decides which program runs when, hands out slices of memory, and stops one app from trampling another. For a PM, the OS matters in two practical ways: it is a platform with its own rules (app store policies, permissions, background-task limits) that constrain what your product can do, and it is a source of fragmentation — different OS versions behave differently, which is why "it works on my phone" is never the whole story.

How software becomes instructions — code and binary

Engineers do not write the 0s and 1s the CPU runs. They write source code in a human-readable programming language (Python, JavaScript, Java, and so on), which is then translated into machine code — the only language the processor truly understands. Wikipedia notes machine code "is classified as native with respect to its host CPU since it is the language that the CPU interprets directly", and all of it bottoms out in binary, since "the binary system is used by almost all modern computers and computer-based devices". The translation happens one of two ways, and the distinction explains a lot of engineering talk: "execution of a program requires an implementation" via either a compiler (translates the whole program ahead of time into a fast, ready-to-run file) or an interpreter (translates and runs line by line, more flexible but often slower). "We need to recompile" or "it's an interpreted language" are now legible phrases.

Networks and IP addresses

For two computers to talk, they need a shared set of rules and a way to find each other. The rules are TCP/IP (the Internet Protocol Suite), a layered stack where each layer handles one job; its famous design principle is to "be conservative in its sending behavior, and liberal in its receiving behavior". The way computers find each other is the IP address — a numerical label identifying a device on the network. The classic framing Wikipedia cites captures why addresses matter: "a name indicates what we seek. An address indicates where it is. A route indicates how to get there". Every site, server, and phone on the internet has one. But humans are bad at remembering numbers like 142.250.72.078 — which is exactly the problem the next piece solves.

DNS — the internet's phone book

You type google.com, not a string of digits. The Domain Name System (DNS) bridges the gap: per Wikipedia it "translates readily memorized domain names to the numerical IP addresses needed for locating and identifying computer services". Think of it as the phone book of the internet — you know the name, DNS looks up the number. This lookup is the silent first step of nearly every online action, and it is a real performance and reliability factor: a slow DNS response delays everything after it, and DNS outages can take down sites whose servers are perfectly healthy. When a PM hears "it was a DNS issue", it usually means users could not even find the server, let alone reach it.

The journey of a web request — the whole trip

Now assemble the pieces into the single most useful picture a PM can hold: what happens between typing a web address and seeing the page. First, DNS turns the domain name into an IP address. Next, the browser opens a connection using TCP (Transmission Control Protocol), which guarantees the data arrives intact — it provides "reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of a stream of octets (bytes) between applications". Then the browser speaks HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol), because "HTTP is a request–response protocol in the client–server model" — it sends a request (often a GET, "please send me this page"). MDN describes the round trip: "the browser sends an HTTP request message to the server, asking it to send a copy of the website to the client. This message... is sent across your internet connection using TCP/IP", and the response comes back as small packets the browser reassembles into the page. Every one of these steps takes time; "latency" is just the sum of these hops, which is why physical distance to the server and the number of round trips both matter to a PM optimizing speed.

Client, server, and the cloud

The whole web rests on a simple division of labor called the client-server model: "clients... initiate communication sessions with servers, which await incoming requests". Your browser or app is the client (it asks); the server answers. A server is not exotic — per Wikipedia it is simply "a computer or software system that provides data, resources, or services to other computers called clients on a computer network". So what is "the cloud"? It is renting those servers from someone else, on demand, instead of buying and running your own. Wikipedia's definition: "a paradigm for enabling network access to a scalable and elastic pool of shareable physical or virtual resources with self-service provisioning and administration on demand". The practical PM consequence is cost and scale: cloud turns big upfront hardware purchases into a usage bill that grows and shrinks with your traffic — which is why "our cloud costs" is now a product conversation, not just an engineering one.

Security basics — HTTPS and encryption

By default, data crossing the internet could be read by anyone in between. HTTPS (HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure) is the fix you see as the padlock in the address bar. It is ordinary HTTP wrapped in encryption: "HTTPS signals the browser to use an added encryption layer of SSL/TLS to protect the traffic". The encryption itself is done by TLS (Transport Layer Security), a protocol that scrambles the connection so only the two ends can read it; per Wikipedia, "its use in securing HTTPS remains the most publicly visible" application of it (source). For a PM, the takeaways are concrete: HTTPS is non-negotiable for any product handling user data (browsers now flag plain HTTP as "not secure"), it protects data in transit but not data sitting on a hacked server, and certificate expiry is a real, recurring operational risk that can break a site overnight.

Concrete next actions you can take this week

  • Trace one real request for your own product. Open your site, and in the browser's developer tools (the "Network" tab) watch the requests fire. You will see DNS, the round trips, and which ones are slow — the abstract picture made concrete.
  • Ask your engineers where your servers physically live and how that maps to where your users are. Distance is latency; this one question often explains a "slow in region X" complaint.
  • Find your product's biggest source of latency — a slow DNS step, a distant server, too many round trips, or a heavy response — and put it on the backlog as a measurable goal.
  • Check that every page is HTTPS and find out who owns certificate renewal. An expired certificate is a preventable outage.
  • Learn the difference your team draws between memory and storage limits for your product, so you can tell a "needs more RAM" problem from a "needs more disk" one.
  • Pick one term you have been nodding along to — "the cloud", "compile", "packet" — and ask an engineer to walk you through it on your actual system. Trade vague comfort for a real model.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Software Engineering & the development lifecycle — how the code that runs on this machinery actually gets built and shipped.
  • APIs & system integration — how one server talks to another, building on the client-server and HTTP foundations here.
  • Performance & latency optimization — turning the request-path picture into measurable speed wins.
  • Cloud infrastructure & cost management — going deeper on what running servers in the cloud actually costs and how it scales.
  • Security & privacy fundamentals — extending HTTPS into authentication, data-at-rest encryption, and threat modeling.
